title: Caching — Making Things Fast
subtitle: Browser cache, CDN, application cache, database cache, and Redis

sections.title: The Cache Layers
sections.text:
Every request that doesn't hit a cache is a wasted opportunity. Caching happens at multiple layers simultaneously:

  1. Browser Cache — The browser stores static assets (images, CSS, JS) locally based on Cache-Control headers. A max-age=31536000 means cache for a year.
  2. CDN Cache — Content Delivery Networks (Cloudflare, Fastly, Akamai) cache responses at edge locations close to the user. Your server handles far fewer requests.
  3. Application Cache — Your server caches computed results (database queries, rendered templates) in memory or Redis. Avoids recomputing expensive operations.
  4. Database Cache — PostgreSQL and MySQL have internal buffer caches for frequently accessed data pages.
sections.caption: Four layers of caching: browser → CDN → app → database
sections.code:
# Cache-Control headers
Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable
Cache-Control: private, max-age=3600
Cache-Control: no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate

# ETag for conditional requests
ETag: "33a64df551425fcc55e4d42a148795d9f25f89d4"
If-None-Match: "33a64df5..."  → 304 Not Modified

sections.title: Cache Invalidation
sections.text:
There are only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things. Invalidating a cache means ensuring stale data isn't served to users.

Common strategies:

  • TTL (Time To Live) — Set an expiration time. Simple but can serve stale data until the TTL expires.
  • Write-through — Update the cache whenever the database is updated. Always fresh but more complex.
  • Cache-aside — Application checks cache first, falls back to database on miss, and updates the cache. Most common pattern.
  • Event-driven — When data changes, publish an event (via Redis pub/sub or message queue) that invalidates related cache keys.

items: No cache → goes to origin server

sections.caption: Cache hit = fast response with no server load. Cache miss = full server processing.
sections.callout: The cache-aside pattern is the most common: check cache first, fall back to database on miss, update cache on read. Invalidate cache on writes.

sections.title: Redis — The Swiss Army Knife of Caching
sections.text:
Redis is an in-memory data store that's incredibly fast (sub-millisecond latency). It's used for caching, session storage, rate limiting, real-time leaderboards, message queues, and more.

Key features: data structures (strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets), TTL with automatic eviction, pub/sub messaging, persistence (optional), and Lua scripting for atomic operations.

Redis is single-threaded (for commands), which makes it predictable. Don't run slow commands like KEYS * in production — use SCAN instead.

items.heading: Hashes
items.body: Object-like: HSET user:1 name Alice. Perfect for cached DB rows.

items.heading: Lists
items.body: Ordered collections. LPUSH/RPUSH for queues, BRPOP for blocking reads.

items.heading: Sorted Sets
items.body: Ranked by score. ZADD/ZRANGE for leaderboards, rate limiting.

items.heading: Pub/Sub
items.body: Real-time messaging. PUBLISH channel message. SUBSCRIBE channel.

items.heading: TTL
items.body: EXPIRE key seconds. Keys auto-delete. Perfect for time-bounded caches.

sections.caption: Redis data structures — each designed for specific use cases
sections.code:
> SET user:1 '{"name":"Alice"}' EX 3600
OK
> GET user:1
"{\"name\":\"Alice\"}"
> INCR page:views:homepage
(integer) 42
> LPUSH queue:tasks 'send-email'
(integer) 1
> BRPOP queue:tasks 0
1) "queue:tasks"
2) "send-email"
